segments.s663-g1: بخندید is a past/simple perfect-form verb meaning 'laughed' (third-person); here it opens the clause and governs the following noun تموز as the logical subject (verb before subject is common in epic Persian). تموز is a personified month/season (the figure doing the laughing). بر + سرخ سیب is a prepositional phrase 'at/on the red apple' with بر marking the target and سرخ (adjective) preceding the noun سیب.
segments.s663-g2: همی‌کرد is the imperfective/habitual past shape (همی- prefix + کرد) giving a sense of repeated or ongoing action 'would do / used to do'. با introduces an instrumental/associative phrase 'with'. بار here denotes 'load/fruit/burden' in the sense of the apple's fruit/weight. و simply coordinates. برگش is the noun برگ with the possessive suffix -ش ('its leaves'); the supplied lemma senses did not include 'leaf' so that specific sense is indicated separately. عتیب is a noun meaning 'reproach' or 'complaint' and functions as the complement of همی‌کرد ('would make/express reproach').
segments.s663-g3: که introduces an explanatory/relative clause here ('for/that'), followed by the demonstrative آن 'that' and the noun phrase دسته گل 'a bunch of flowers' (دسته before گل, as in classical Persian). بوقت بهار is a temporal phrase 'at the time of spring' (بوقت = به وقت ‘at the time of’ + بهار 'spring'); the provided sense list for بوقت did not match this temporal reading, so that sense is marked separately.
segments.s663-g4: بمستی is an adverbial phrase 'in drunkenness/inebriation' (بِ + مستی). همی‌داشتی is the imperfective/past-habitual second-person form (همی- + داشتی) meaning 'you used to hold/kept' or 'you had (beside you)'. درکنار is a locative prepositional phrase 'beside' or 'in the company of' indicating where the flowers were kept; the direct object of همی‌داشتی is understood to be آن دسته گل from the previous segment.
segments.s663-g5: همی here functions as an emphatic/continuous particle (classical usage) preceding the noun phrase. باد is the noun 'wind' (a poetic image: 'a wind/breeze of shame'). شرم is the noun 'shame/modesty' and آمد is the past verb 'came' placed after the subject phrase, as is normal in classical Persian clauses. از marks source 'from', رنگ here means 'complexion/appearance', and اوی is the enclitic/old possessive form 'his/her', so 'from his complexion.'
segments.s663-g6: The second clause mirrors the first in structure: همی as emphatic/continuous marker, یاد یار 'the memory of the beloved' is subject, آمد 'came' is the past verb, از indicates source, چنگ is 'harp/lyre' (a poetic instrument) and اوی again is the possessive 'his/her' — 'the memory of the beloved came from his harp.' Word order is subject then verb, typical in Ferdowsi's diction.
segments.s663-g7: چه کردی is a direct interrogative 'what did you do' (چه + past 2nd-person کردی). که introduces the result or circumstance clause 'that' (i.e., 'that there was for you...'). بودت is the past form with the enclitic -ت 'you' ('you were/had'), here functioning like 'you had' or 'there was to you'; خریدار is the noun 'buyer' serving as predicate complement in the result clause, and آن is the demonstrative 'that' (the thing referred back to). The sequence asks how it came about that you found a buyer.
segments.s663-g8: کجا is the interrogative 'where' and introduces this follow-up question. یافتی is the past tense second-person of یافتن 'you found/obtained'. تیز here modifies بازار: in context it means 'quick/swift/brisk' (a bustling or eager market), so تیز بازار means a market where things sell quickly; بازار is 'market', and the final آن is the demonstrative 'for that' (referring to the item in question). The clause literally asks 'Where did you find a swift market for that?'
segments.s663-g9: A relative clause headed by که (that) modifies عقیق و زبرجد. دادت is the archaic/poetic past verb 'gave' with the enclitic -ت marking the object 'you' (i.e. 'gave you'); بهم here means 'together' (not 'to me'). Word order is relative-clause final (gems + که + verb + adverb), as is common in classical Persian.
segments.s663-g10: This is an ablative phrase introduced by ز ('from') indicating cause: 'from the heavy load'. شاخ تو is the noun + possessive enclitic 'your horn/branch' (شاخ + تو). هم is the adverb 'also/too' modifying the following predicate. بخم is an archaic/obscure word (poetic) that functions as the predicate here — the verb-like or adjectival result applying to شاخ تو (hence translation as 'was bowed' or similar).
segments.s663-g11: همانا is an emphatic adverb 'verily/indeed'. که here has a causal sense 'since/because' introducing the reason clause. گل را is the definite object marker construction ('the rose'), بها is 'price/value', and خواستی is the past tense 2nd person 'you desired/sought'.
segments.s663-g12: بدان is a fused prepositional expression 'به + آن' = 'with that/by that' (classical usage) introducing the means. رنگ رخ را is a noun phrase 'the color of the face' with را marking the definite object. بیاراستی here is the poetic past form meaning 'you adorned' (bi- + آراستی); note that the supplied sense list does not reflect this verbal reading, so the verb is treated as the main predicate of the clause.
segments.s663-g13: همی is an iterative/adverbial particle 'continually/still'. رنگ here has the sense 'complexion/complexion-color', and شرم 'shame' yields 'a blush of shame'. آید is the present/past-poetic verb 'comes' (here 'rises/appears'), and از گردنت is the source phrase 'from your neck' (از + noun + possessive).
segments.s663-g14: Parallel structure to the previous clause: همی (again 'continually'), مشک 'musk' as the source of scent, بوید is the intransitive verb 'gives off a smell / emits' (here third-person present/past-poetic), and ز پیراهنت is the source ('from your shirt'). Word order is subject–verb–source.
segments.s663-g15: مگر introduces an exception/clause 'unless' (classical use). جامه 'garment' is the direct object/topic, از مشتری literally 'from/because of Jupiter' (i.e. under the influence or on account of the planet Jupiter), and بستدی is a poetic past form 'you fastened/closed' (archaic ending -ی). The clause reads as a counterfactual conditional ('unless you had...').
segments.s663-g16: This clause gives the consequence that would have followed: به introduces the instrument/means ('with' or 'by'), لوئلؤ 'pearls', بر is the locative/prepositional 'on/upon', از خون نقط 'with spots/dots of blood' (از introduces the material/source), and برزدی is an archaic/poetic verb form giving the resulting action (here 'you would have placed/marked'—the exact nuance is archaic). Word order places the instrument before the verb as is common in poetry.
segments.s663-g25: چورنگت here is a fused expression (چو + رنگ + -ت) meaning “when/if your color”; شود is the present/future sense of شدن “become”; سبز is the predicative adjective “green”; بستایمت is a first‑person verb with the enclitic object -ت (“you”) from ستودن/ستاییدن, a classical form meaning “I praise/shall praise you.” The clause thus reads literally “when your color becomes green, I will praise you.”
segments.s663-g26: چو is the subordinating particle “when/as/if”; دیهیم qualifies هرمز as “rustic/country” (a descriptive epithet); هرمز is used as a personal name here; بیارایمت is the first‑person verb بیاراییدن/آراییدن with enclitic -ت (“I will/shall adorn you”), a classical present/subjunctive form used for a future or prospective action. The clause means “when (the) rustic Hormoz adorns you, I will embellish you.”
segments.s663-g27: که introduces an explanatory/subordinate clause (“that/for”); امروز is the temporal adverb “today”; تیزست is the contracted classical form of تیز است (“is sharp/keen”) where -ست = است; بازار من is the noun + possessive pronoun (“my market”), with the possessor following the noun as is normal in Persian.
segments.s663-g28: نبینی is the negative second‑person present/subjunctive form meaning “you do not see / you will not see” (used here to state a consequence for the future); پس از is the compound prepositional phrase “after” (پس + از); مرگ is “death”; آثار من is “my آثار,” here best understood as the remaining traces or works of the speaker. The phrase reads “you will not see my traces after my death.”
segments.s663-g17: زبرجدت is زبرجد + -ت, a possessive clitic: “your peridot/green gem.” The clause is verbless/elliptical: برگست functions as a predicative element describing the gem (an archaic or poetic form here, see note). و links this clause to چرمت بنفش, where چرمت (“your leather”) plus the adjective بنفش (“purple”) form another nominal predicate with the copula omitted, a common poetic structure.
segments.s663-g18: سرت is سر + -ت, “your head.” برتر is a comparative adjective placed after the noun and takes از to introduce the standard of comparison; so سرت برتر از X = “your head is higher than X.” کاویانی درفش is a genitival phrase “the Kayanian banner/standard.” The copula (“is”) is again omitted.
segments.s663-g19: بپیرایه is a prepositional/adverbial adjective built with ب- (with) + پیرایه (adornment), meaning “with adornment/ornamented.” The three colors زرد و سرخ و سپید are coordinated by و; they specify the colors of the adornment and follow the prepositional phrase.
segments.s663-g20: مرا کردی is object + past-tense verb: مرا (me, object) precedes کردی (you made/did), so “you made me [such-and-such].” از برگ گل ناامید is a prepositional phrase where از = “about/of/concerning,” برگ گل = “the leaf of the flower” (genitive), and ناامید is a predicate meaning “hopeless.” Together: “you made me hopeless concerning the flower’s leaf.”
segments.s663-g21: نگارا and بهارا are vocatives (addressing the beloved and spring); classical Persian often stacks vocatives. کجا رفته‌ای is a direct interrogative: کجا “where” + رفته‌ای (second-person perfect/past participle with enclitic -ī) = “where have you gone?”
segments.s663-g22: که introduces a subordinate clause—“that/which.” آرایش باغ is a noun phrase “the garden’s adornment” (آرایش = adornment, باغ = garden). بنهفته‌ای is an archaic verbal/adjectival form: بنهفته (hidden) with a second-person ending -ی, giving the sense “you have hidden” or “thou hast hidden/caused to be hidden,” so the clause reads “that you have hidden the garden’s adornment.”
segments.s663-g23: همی is an adverb meaning “always/still” in classical Persian. مهرگان is the subject (the festival/time of Mehregan). بوید is a present/stative verb meaning “smells/is scented/emanates” (third-person). از باد تو is a prepositional phrase “from your breeze,” with تو as possessive.
segments.s663-g24: بجام is an adverbial phrase “in/into the cup” (ب- + جام) placed before the verb. می‌اندر کن��م is a compound classical-form verb: می‌اندر (within/into) + کنم (1st-person singular of کردن) giving the sense “I will put/enter.” یاد تو is the object “your memory/remembrance.” The order (adverbial – verb – object) is typical of poetic word order; کن��م here expresses the speaker’s action/intent (“I put / I will put”).

sentences.s663-s1: Tammuz laughed at the red apple, and (it) would complain with its load and leaves.
sentences.s663-s2: For that bunch of flowers, in the time of spring, you used to keep (them) beside you in drunkenness.
sentences.s663-s3: A wind of shame came from his complexion, and the memory of the beloved came from his lute.
sentences.s663-s4: What did you do that you had a buyer for that? Where did you find a brisk market for it?
sentences.s663-s5: The agate and the peridot that were given to you together — from the heavy burden even your horn was bowed.
sentences.s663-s6: Indeed, since you wanted a price for the rose, you adorned the face with that color.
sentences.s663-s7: A constant blush of shame rises from your neck; your shirt continually gives off the scent of musk.
sentences.s663-s8: Unless you had fastened the garment because of Jupiter, you would have set pearls upon it and dotted it with drops of blood.
sentences.s663-s13: When your hue becomes green I will praise you; when the rustic Hormoz adorns you I will embellish you.
sentences.s663-s14: For my market is brisk today; you will not see my traces after my death.
sentences.s663-s9: Your peridot is like a leaf and your leather is purple; your head is loftier than the Kayanian banner.
sentences.s663-s10: You have adorned (it) with yellow, red, and white, and you have made me hopeless about the flower’s leaf.
sentences.s663-s11: O beauty, O spring, where have you gone, that you have hidden the garden’s adornment?
sentences.s663-s12: Every Mehregan still breathes of your breeze; I will put your memory into the cup.
